speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 23, 1996. We're interviewing Estelle Laughlin, maiden name Wakszlak.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Esther Finder. We're in Kensington, Maryland. And the language is English. Today is October 23, 1996. We're interviewing Estelle Laughlin, maiden name Wakszlak. My name is Esther Finder. We're in Kensington, Maryland. And the language is English. I'd like to thank you for doing the interview with us today. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Estelle Laughlin, L-A-U-G-H-L-I-N. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name is Wakszlak, W-A-K-S-Z-L-A-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your first name Estelle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And my Jewish name is Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in July, 1929. I was born in Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am 67 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up in Warsaw, Poland--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Warsaw like?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and in concentration camps [LAUGHS] and in the Warsaw Ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Warsaw like in your childhood days?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you first a little bit about my family. And then I'll share with you our street and what life was like. We were a family of four-- my mother, my father, and my sister, who's a year and a half older. My mother and my sister survived with me-- survived the war with me. We lived in Warsaw. My father was a jeweler. He worked out of his-- out of our home, which made him always available to us. So was his wisdom and his kindness. My mother did not work outside the home. My mother was a very-- a, a person who loved life a lot. And she was a very vibrant person. She-- her, her laughter just cascaded out of her. In fact, she-- I think that she was as full of sadness as she was full of joy. And my mother really is an example of the Diaspora. Because I know that many people often think of the suffering of the Jews as being very distant, like 2,000 years ago. To me, it's very immediate and very continuous, because my mother was born in Russia-- in White Russia. And she experienced two horrendous wars-- the First World War. I don't know whether it was prior to the war or during the war, but she was-- she was chased out of Russia by the pogroms. And, and she suffered tremendous hunger and dislocation and persecution. Then she had a few good years, relatively good years. And then the second war came. My mother imbued us with a love for beauty, a love for life. My mother never passed a flower or a cloud in the sky that she would not-- oh, how beautiful it is! Oh, how wonderful it is! She did not have much formal education. But she was very much involved in what was going on. She loved the opera. And she would-- I remember when she would go to the opera, she would just float into the house and just sing the arias and tell us-- usually it was the morning after-- and tell us the stories the-- of, of the operas word by word, action by action. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We lived-- we were really ordinary people-- middle-class people. We practiced the typical, good, middle-class values. There was a lot of love in our immediate family and surrounding and extended family. We lived on a nice, middle-class street. In the street-- the houses on our street-- well, actually, they were buildings. Warsaw was an ancient city. I say 'was' because you know that Warsaw was totally, totally destroyed. And so whatever we see now, what people see now in Warsaw as the old city is really a city on top of a city. It really-- whatever was isn't anymore. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The houses on our street stood sort of like shoulder to shoulder, and they formed long blocks. The-- we had-- most of the houses had big iron gates. Now, I don't know if all of them had big iron gates. But this is what stands out in my memory. And I think it's because they bring back very pleasant memories. They kind of give me an illusion of security and yet not isolation because you could look right through them. I loved the clanging of the-- of the gates when they were closed. Beyond-- beyond-- behind the gate was a large, rectangular courtyard in the-- which was surrounded by four buildings that stood at right angles to one another. And in the center was a little garden, a fenced-in garden. And the rest of the yard-- of the courtyard was paved. And that's where the children played, under the windows where they could not escape the supervision of their parents. Generally, our streets were very safe. And I remember with enormous fondness our holidays and, and the warmth and security of the community, friendly neighbors, holidays when we would gather together, play with cousins and friends and hear conversations of adults. And they were all wonderful memories. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] However, outside my immediate neighborhood-- we lived in a Jewish section of town-- outside my immediate neighborhood, when I reflect on it, I-- and mixed feelings come back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Every-- not every Saturday, but very often, my-- we would go to the park for a walk. Now, I-- Warsaw was a beautiful city. It was my city. It was my home. It was my center. It was all I knew, and I loved it. That's all I knew. And I loved the parks. Yet I remember that in my parks, when I would go for a walk with my parents, suddenly, there would be an outbreak of panic. And you would see people just running. And you would hear this, this wild laughter of pursuers. And they were hooligans who were attacking Jews in the park, my park, where I fed my swans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also remember the tremendous fear and shock that, that followed me when I heard on the radio about stabbings in these parks of Jews, which also was  not uncommon. I don't know how often they happened. They probably were quite sporadic. But they left a tremendous imprint, tremendous scars, on me then and even now. I also remember that when I was very young-- when I was in first grade-- and when I, I used to break up in hives regularly. My mother took me to doctors. And all I remember that they could suggest to remedy it was for my mother to sponge my body to relieve the itch and, and, and, and, and the irritation. What happened was that when I used to go to school, we used to have to cross a Christian section. And I don't know how many times-- but certainly too many-- some of the children chased us. I remember running in panic. And I remember the parents who sat there and laughed because they were amused at our fear and our panic. That really added to my fear. Because look, I couldn't even count on the judgment of the adults. I couldn't even count that they would protect me. After that, my mother, we, we were-- walked to school. But nevertheless, the faces still seemed so mean and just staring at us. And they were right there. In spite of all of that, I-- when I think of my childhood, everything seems to be just absolutely drenched in golden radiance. It's a very special time for me. It really was my core. This, this is where I learned safety. This is where I learned love. This is where I learned joy for life. This is where I learned who I was, and who I must remain. And really, it served me, I think, as a very, very important tool for survival, I think. I'm saying that in, in reflecting as an adult woman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how conscious it was. It probably was not. But I do remember distinctly how we all conjured up-- even when we were in the barracks or under the worst of circumstances, we conjured up our past, that we remembered the warmth and the wonderfulness of home. And I think that that very much carried us through and laid the core in us of humanity. And I think, I hope, we passed it on to our progeny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religiously observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was-- had a very strong identification of Jewishness, but they were not-- they were not observing it regularly. We had Shabbat. Saturday was special. On holidays, often we went-- well, not very often, but on high holidays sometimes we did go to the temple. The times that stand out in my mind especially is during Symcha's Torah because I loved the, the, the, the little flags with the apple on top. And it was such a joyful holiday, that I remember we did go to-- these are the times that I recall at the temple most vividly, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had very little formal schooling. And really, my formal schooling began as an adult in the United States when I started to go to the university. Between that and my schooling as a child was-- I had three years of formal education, first, second, and third grade. And then the invasion. And schools were closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a public school, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any education in, in Jewish tradition? Or did you go to Hebrew school or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took private Hebrew lessons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism in school from your classmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: It seems to me that most of the children in my-- in my school were Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember for certain. I cannot tell for certain whether the schools were segregated. I think probably not because I do have a recollection of some non-Jewish children at school. The teachers-- some were Jewish, some were not. I also recall that the non-Jewish children were-- had also religious classes, which we did not have. But only the non-Jewish children were called out-- pulled out of the classroom for that purpose.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for fun when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: We played a lot. We played dress-up, holidays, Purims. And, and we played in the street a lot, in the courtyard a lot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just did the same things that my children and all children do in the United States. We played hopscotch and, and volleyball and all these wonderful things that children do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a special friend in your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --building or your courtyard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had a very special friend. Her name was Janka. She was very, very special to me. We were very, very close. She lived right below us. And her room was right below the bedroom where I slept. And very often we would communicate through the floor. But we were inseparable. And she was very, very precious to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you care to mention for the tape your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father's name was Symcha. My mother's name was Michla.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frieda.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very young. But did you have any understanding of the politics that were going on around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was an awareness. My parents were very much involved in and, and, and, and cared about what was going on in the world and around them. And so there were always discussions. We, as children, were very much respected and very much involved in what was going on. So the rumors of war were very, very clear, except that we did not quite understand them. Because to us, as children, there was a mixture of fear and a mixture of adventure. War was like a game. It was out there. It, it, it, it-- it's not real.

speaker: interviewer
text: So yes, there was an awareness and, and the unrest of, of, of outbreaks of hooliganism. All of that was very, very clear and very much part of our lives, just as much as the joyous parts were. And really, very much in my life is like, like balancing on a very, very sharp edge of wonderfulness and horror. [LAUGHS] When you think, think back to those days, do you, by any chance, recall some of your images of, of Hitler and what  the Nazis were doing? You know, what was your understanding?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- I-- I'm talking now about pre-war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Before, in the '30s--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when Hitler came to power in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: The news that came, this getting ready to invade, the justifications or the rationalizations, the exclamations, the declarations of superiority, the put-downs of not just Jewish people, but-- everyone else was a secondary citizen. The Jews were below anything. The Poles were dirty. The English were something else. But everyone-- there, there was a great-- I think that the build-up towards all of the actions that the Germans did that came out in the news and, and, and the justifications that surely they had a right because they needed the lebensraum, the room that, that they lost. There were all kinds during the First World War. There were all kinds of justifications and rationalizations and signs of aggression that we were aware of in the news. But it still was out there. It still was not, not real. And it was, you know-- and then there were all kinds of preparations by the Polish people. There were all kinds of slogans on the radio. So it was-- there really was a, a psychological preparation. There were slogans. We are fortified and strong. And nobody will ever overrun Poland. And if the Germans will come, there's no way that they'll come in. And we are better. And we are superior. And they are not going-- so this preparation, the build-up, was very-- a drumming up of patriotism. There was a great deal of, of, of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jewish community get wrapped up in that patriotism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, but you see, the Jewish-- the patriot-- whether the Jews were wrapped up in the patriotism, in a sense, in a sense, yes. But, but I don't know what this word connotes. And I want to, to, to explain. The Jewish people were Polish people. They were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for us, this was our home. This was-- this was-- this was our safety. So that we were set apart, it's almost like being orphaned.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, when people say-- after I, I share my stories about how I was treated as a child and they say, do you still feel Polish? And I say, yes and no. I-- no, I am definitely Jewish. And definitely I am not Polish because they didn't let me be Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then I was Polish. Then I, I felt-- I, I felt an equal claim. I was-- I feel that I was kicked out of my home. But this was my home. This was my beautiful city. This is-- this is where I-- where, where-- this was my sky. These were my trees. This, this, this was everything that, that fed my soul. So yes, in that sense, that was mine. And, and of course, when the country was threatened, all of us were threatened. In that sense, sure, the Jewish people were mobilized, too. Because there was a mobilization. And my cousins-- and, you know, some of them who were old enough were drafted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in that sense, there was a, a patriotism. There was an identification. But we never, never were-- we never forgot that, that there was a separation. And there was no escape for us. There was no way to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I think that my mother's history, in a way, justifies-- more than justifies but cries out for the existence of an Israel. Because Jews were constantly-- I, I don't think that American people or American Jews can quite taste what it means to be a Jew. See, Europe is made up of small countries. And they are very homogeneous. Outsiders are not-- are not well accepted. We were seen as outsiders. And so my mother's being kicked out of her home in Russia during the pogroms, and, and, and even now, you, you, you, you read about outbursts and you hear about outbursts of anti-Semitism. And, and you know, I feel so completely American. This is where-- this is where I was born again. This is where I was-- this is where I was a normal human being. This is where I could return to myself. I love this country. I think I appreciate it more. I appreciate the freedom. I  will never ever miss the opportunity to vote. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To me, it, it-- it's such a gift. But yet I don't escape the fear and the knowledge that there is a place-- Israel-- where I will always find a, a safety. It's important. I don't know. I know it's not rational.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's just as irrational as I sometimes will wake up in the middle of the night and think of, where could I hide? I don't need to hide anymore. But it's amazing. I, I, I know every place under every cushion that I can crawl in to. [CRYING] Not rational, but it's real. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Can you describe for me the beginning of the war from your eyes? How old were you? And what do you remember from that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I just turned 10. My-- I turned, turned 10-- turned-- in July. And the war started in September. I remember the propaganda. I, I remember the, the, the drumming up of the war, the, the, the, the preparation that I mentioned before-- the, the, the possibility of-- that the Germans are building up and threatening the invasion of Poland, and on the radios, trying to prepare and fortify. And, and, and, and then the bombings started. And then, oh, we had these trial alarms, like sirens. And the sirens were repeated, the, the trial sirens, that when the real sirens sounded we didn't-- we didn't take it seriously. And I remember that my father was away on business-- not out of the city, but was not home. And he came back. And he said, oh, it was real. Because the first bomb was dropped not far from where he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in spite of the preparation, the reality just crashed in on us. And then the bombardments, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the bombardments-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- then, then the bombardments started. And that-- a little incident that is coming to my mind, yes? I'm going to stop you, so we can change tape. And then you'll tell me the incident.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK.
